params.text:
What to check when someone has fallen

If you find someone on the floor, stay calm — your response can prevent further harm. Do not rush to lift them. First, check the situation.

  • Response and breathing: are they conscious and breathing normally? If they are unresponsive or not breathing normally, call 999 and start your emergency procedure.
  • Injury and pain: ask where it hurts and look for signs of a fracture, such as a shortened or turned-out leg, or an obvious deformity.
  • What happened: if they can tell you, find out whether they felt dizzy, blacked out or hit their head.
  • Comfort: keep them warm and reassured while you decide what to do.

Never assume a fall is minor just because the person says they are fine.

params.alt: Illustration for After a fall in depth

params.text:
When to call 999, and never lifting by hand

Call 999 for an ambulance if any of these apply, even if the person plays it down:

  • Head injury: they hit their head, or seem drowsy, confused or unwell afterwards.
  • Anticoagulants: they take blood-thinning medicines such as warfarin, apixaban or rivaroxaban, as these can cause dangerous bleeding, including inside the head, after a knock.
  • Suspected fracture: severe pain, an inability to move or bear weight, or a limb that looks wrong.
  • Unresponsive, unwell, or you are simply unsure.

Crucially, if someone may be injured, do not move them, and never lift them from the floor by hand. Manual lifting risks worsening a fracture or head injury and can injure you too. If it is safe to raise an uninjured person, use suitable equipment, such as a raising cushion or hoist, as you have been trained. Then record and report the fall so its cause can be reviewed.

params.question: You find Mr Adeyemi on his bedroom floor. He says he simply slipped and feels fine, but he takes apixaban and has a small graze on his forehead. What should you do?

answers.text: Do not lift him; keep him still and warm, and call 999, because a knock to the head while on blood-thinning medication can cause dangerous bleeding.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. A head injury in someone taking an anticoagulant is an emergency even if they feel well; he must not be moved and needs urgent medical assessment.

answers.text: Help him straight up off the floor, since he says he feels fine and the graze looks small.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. He has hit his head while on a blood thinner; lifting him and assuming he is fine could miss a serious, hidden bleed.

answers.text: Give him a cup of tea and note it in the diary, as there is no need to involve anyone else.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. This situation needs a 999 call, not just a diary note; delaying could let a dangerous bleed go untreated.

answers.text: Wait a few hours to see whether he develops a headache before deciding whether to call for help.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Waiting is dangerous with a head injury on anticoagulants; you should call 999 straight away rather than watch and hope.
